title: Drowning / Near-Drowning
oneLiner: Respiratory impairment from submersion/immersion in liquid; leading cause of accidental death; survival depends on rapid reversal of hypoxia, hypothermia, and spinal injury.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe hypothermia (<28°C) + bradycardia/asystole** — core temp <30°C causes myocardial irritability; single finding: core temp <28°C + unresponsive pupils → do not assume death until rewarmed; requires active rewarming before declaring futility.
- **Cervical spine injury (especially diving/fall from height)** — single finding: flaccid limbs with no spontaneous movement + diaphragmatic breathing → immobilise immediately; do not miss by treating as routine drowned patient.
- **Aspiration pneumonitis / early ARDS** — single finding: frothy pink sputum + diffuse crackles on auscultation + severe hypoxia (SpO2 <85% on high-flow O2) → airway protection and early ventilation needed; not just “wet lung”.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** cough, dyspnoea, frothy sputum (pink), confusion/agitation, cyanosis, shivering/hypothermia
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Respiratory: tachypnoea, crackles/wheezes, reduced air entry, use of accessory muscles
    - Cardiovascular: tachycardia initially, then bradycardia (hypothermia), hypotension
    - Neurological: GCS <15, seizures, fixed/dilated pupils (if hypothermia → reassess after rewarming)
    - Skin: cool, pale, wet clothing
    - Oropharynx: water/debris, vomit, laryngospasm signs (submersion + no air entry)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:**  
    - Aspiration pneumonitis (no fever, rapid onset)  
    - Aspiration pneumonia (later, >24h, fever, purulent sputum)  
    - Hypothermia (core temp <35°C, shivering or obtunded)  
- **Less likely:**  
    - Traumatic head injury (focal neurology, scalp wound, not resolving after rewarming)  
    - Seizure causing submersion (tonic-clonic history, post-ictal confusion)  
    - Cardiac arrhythmia / ACS (chest pain, ECG changes, troponin rise)  
    - Drug or alcohol intoxication (history, odour, toxidrome)  
- **Splitter:** Frothy pink sputum + diffuse pulmonary oedema on CXR without fever → drowning pneumonitis, not pneumonia
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
    - Core temperature (tympanic/esophageal if intubated)  
    - SpO₂, ECG monitoring  
    - Blood glucose (fingerstick)  
    - Capillary refill, BP, pulse  
    - Consider spinal immobilisation until spinal injury excluded (CT/MRI)
- **Bloods:**  
    - ABG (early hypoxaemia, acidosis, hypercapnia if ventilator)  
    - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFT  
    - Coagulation screen (if DIC suspected)  
    - Lactate  
    - Cardiac troponin (if rhythm change or hypotension)  
    - Ethanol/toxicology screen (if altered consciousness not explained)  
    - Blood cultures (if fever develops)
- **Imaging:**  
    - First-line: Chest X-ray (pulmonary oedema, aspiration, pneumothorax)  
    - Second-line: CT head/c-spine (if unconscious, focal signs, known trauma)  
    - Consider: CT chest if ARDS or ventilation needed
- **Special tests:**  
    - ECG (look for J waves, bradycardia, atrial fibrillation – hypothermia)  
    - Continuous core temp monitoring  
    - Consider bronchoscopy if suspected foreign body (rare)
- **Never-miss test:** Core temperature (hypothermia can mimic death; rewarm before declaring brain death)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
    - **ABCDE:** Airway assessment – intubate if GCS <8, agonal breathing, severe hypoxia  
    - **Oxygen:** 100% high-flow (non-rebreather) until SpO₂ >94%, then titrate  
    - **Spinal precautions:** full immobilisation until cleared (CT/MRI) if mechanism suggests diving/fall  
    - **Rapid rewarming:** remove wet clothes, warm blankets, warm IV fluids (40°C), forced air warming  
    - **Ventilation:** Lung-protective strategy (tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, PEEP 5-10 cmH₂O)  
    - **Hypotension:** IV crystalloid 20 mL/kg (warmed), reassess; use inotropes if refractory  
    - **IV access:** two wide-bore lines (warm fluids)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
    - Monitor for ARDS (P/F ratio, CXR, O₂ saturations)  
    - Reassess for aspiration pneumonia – sputum culture, consider antibiotics if signs of infection  
    - Neurological monitoring (GCS, pupils) every hour – consider CT head if deterioration  
    - Repeat ABG after rewarming (metabolic acidosis may resolve)  
    - Maintain normothermia (avoid hyperthermia – brain damage risk)  
    - Electrolyte correction (especially K+, Mg²⁺)  
    - Anxiety/sedation if combative – consider benzodiazepines (watch respiratory depression)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
    - Respiratory follow-up for pulmonary fibrosis (rare)  
    - Neuropsychological assessment if prolonged hypoxia  
    - Cardiac follow-up if arrhythmia or troponin rise  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
    - Fluids: Warmed 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s at maintenance (30 mL/kg/day, adjust for fever) – avoid cold fluids  
    - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if no contraindication – hypothermia may impair clearance)  
    - Diet: NBM until airway reflexes assessed (if extubated, clear fluids then full diet)  
    - Observation: 4-hourly vital signs (core temp, SpO₂, GCS, urine output) – NEWS2 chart
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**  
    - *Antibiotics:* If aspiration pneumonia confirmed (fever, purulent sputum, new infiltrate): Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS [or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS – check local policy]  
    - *Bronchodilators:* Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (adult) PRN if wheeze – [dose per local protocol]  
    - *Rewarming:* Warm IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1L warmed to 40°C) – no specific drug dose  
- **Common alternatives:**  
    - *Antibiotics:* If penicillin allergy – levofloxacin 500 mg IV OD or [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
    - Hypothermia reduces drug clearance – adjust doses (e.g., enoxaparin, sedatives) cautiously  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
    - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose if CrCl <30  
    - Piperacillin-tazobactam: adjust for renal impairment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
    - **Rewarming too rapidly** (not a drug but a management error) – can cause rewarming shock, arrhythmias; target 0.5–1°C/hour  
    - **Giving cold IV fluids** – worsens hypothermia; always warm fluids before infusion
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
    - ARDS (2–48h) – high mortality  
    - Aspiration pneumonia (24–72h)  
    - Cerebral oedema / raised ICP (if prolonged hypoxia)  
    - Cardiac arrhythmia (hypothermia-related, reperfusion)  
    - Acute kidney injury (hypoperfusion, rhabdomyolysis)  
    - DIC (rare, from acidosis/hypothermia)  
- **Chronic:**  
    - Pulmonary fibrosis (rare, after severe ARDS)  
    - Neurocognitive deficits (memory, executive function)  
    - Persistent pulmonary function abnormalities (restrictive pattern)
sections.Prognosis:
- Overall mortality ~15–30%; higher if submersion >5 min, core temp <28°C, GCS <5 on arrival, or need for CPR >25 min  
- Good neurological outcome: 50–80% in children with short submersion and rapid rewarming; adults worse  
- Surviving the first 24h → 80% eventual discharge (but chronic lung/neuro issues possible)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Assume spinal injury until proven** – immobilise all unconscious drowning patients (especially diving/fall)  
- **Rewarm aggressively but carefully** – aim 1–2°C per hour for severe hypothermia; use forced air, warm IV fluids, lavage if necessary  
- **Check blood glucose early** – hypoglycaemia worsen hypoxia; treat with IV 50% glucose 50 mL (caution in hypothermia – give warmed)  
- **Take a history of co-ingestions** – alcohol, drugs can mimic or cause drowning; toxicology screen mandatory if GCS not improving
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not stop resuscitation based on cold, dilated pupils** – hypothermia protects brain; continue ALS/rewarming until core >30°C  
- **Do not give steroids for aspiration** – NICE/RCUK recommends against dexamethasone/hydrocortisone; no proven benefit, may increase infection risk  
- **Do not give prophylactic antibiotics** – only start if clinical signs of infection (fever, purulent sputum, raised WCC, new infiltrate)  
- **Do not use cold fluids for IV access** – worsens hypothermia; always warm crystalloids
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
    - Shortness of breath worsening, fever, coughing up blood/purulent sputum  
    - New confusion, drowsiness, seizure, weakness  
    - Chest pain or palpitations  
- **Follow-up:**  
    - GP in 48–72h for respiratory assessment; if persistent cough/spO₂ <94% arrange outpatient CXR  
    - Consider referral to respiratory medicine (2-week wait if abnormal CXR at follow-up)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU:**  
    - GCS <13 or ≥2-point drop (neurology review)  
    - SpO₂ <90% on 10 L/min O₂ or FiO₂ >0.5  
    - Need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation  
    - Core temp <32°C (ICU rewarming)  
    - Haemodynamic instability (BP <90/60, lactate >4 mmol/L)  
    - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained score ≥5 despite treatment  
- **When to call crash team:**  
    - Cardiac arrest (asystole is not unrecoverable – start ALS, continue rewarming)  
    - Seizure requiring airway protection  
    - Acute respiratory failure

rawMarkdown:
# Drowning / Near-Drowning

**One-liner:** Respiratory impairment from submersion/immersion in liquid; leading cause of accidental death; survival depends on rapid reversal of hypoxia, hypothermia, and spinal injury.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe hypothermia (<28°C) + bradycardia/asystole** — core temp <30°C causes myocardial irritability; single finding: core temp <28°C + unresponsive pupils → do not assume death until rewarmed; requires active rewarming before declaring futility.
- **Cervical spine injury (especially diving/fall from height)** — single finding: flaccid limbs with no spontaneous movement + diaphragmatic breathing → immobilise immediately; do not miss by treating as routine drowned patient.
- **Aspiration pneumonitis / early ARDS** — single finding: frothy pink sputum + diffuse crackles on auscultation + severe hypoxia (SpO2 <85% on high-flow O2) → airway protection and early ventilation needed; not just “wet lung”.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** cough, dyspnoea, frothy sputum (pink), confusion/agitation, cyanosis, shivering/hypothermia
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Respiratory: tachypnoea, crackles/wheezes, reduced air entry, use of accessory muscles
    - Cardiovascular: tachycardia initially, then bradycardia (hypothermia), hypotension
    - Neurological: GCS <15, seizures, fixed/dilated pupils (if hypothermia → reassess after rewarming)
    - Skin: cool, pale, wet clothing
    - Oropharynx: water/debris, vomit, laryngospasm signs (submersion + no air entry)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:**  
    - Aspiration pneumonitis (no fever, rapid onset)  
    - Aspiration pneumonia (later, >24h, fever, purulent sputum)  
    - Hypothermia (core temp <35°C, shivering or obtunded)  
- **Less likely:**  
    - Traumatic head injury (focal neurology, scalp wound, not resolving after rewarming)  
    - Seizure causing submersion (tonic-clonic history, post-ictal confusion)  
    - Cardiac arrhythmia / ACS (chest pain, ECG changes, troponin rise)  
    - Drug or alcohol intoxication (history, odour, toxidrome)  
- **Splitter:** Frothy pink sputum + diffuse pulmonary oedema on CXR without fever → drowning pneumonitis, not pneumonia

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
    - Core temperature (tympanic/esophageal if intubated)  
    - SpO₂, ECG monitoring  
    - Blood glucose (fingerstick)  
    - Capillary refill, BP, pulse  
    - Consider spinal immobilisation until spinal injury excluded (CT/MRI)
- **Bloods:**  
    - ABG (early hypoxaemia, acidosis, hypercapnia if ventilator)  
    - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFT  
    - Coagulation screen (if DIC suspected)  
    - Lactate  
    - Cardiac troponin (if rhythm change or hypotension)  
    - Ethanol/toxicology screen (if altered consciousness not explained)  
    - Blood cultures (if fever develops)
- **Imaging:**  
    - First-line: Chest X-ray (pulmonary oedema, aspiration, pneumothorax)  
    - Second-line: CT head/c-spine (if unconscious, focal signs, known trauma)  
    - Consider: CT chest if ARDS or ventilation needed
- **Special tests:**  
    - ECG (look for J waves, bradycardia, atrial fibrillation – hypothermia)  
    - Continuous core temp monitoring  
    - Consider bronchoscopy if suspected foreign body (rare)
- **Never-miss test:** Core temperature (hypothermia can mimic death; rewarm before declaring brain death)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
    - **ABCDE:** Airway assessment – intubate if GCS <8, agonal breathing, severe hypoxia  
    - **Oxygen:** 100% high-flow (non-rebreather) until SpO₂ >94%, then titrate  
    - **Spinal precautions:** full immobilisation until cleared (CT/MRI) if mechanism suggests diving/fall  
    - **Rapid rewarming:** remove wet clothes, warm blankets, warm IV fluids (40°C), forced air warming  
    - **Ventilation:** Lung-protective strategy (tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, PEEP 5-10 cmH₂O)  
    - **Hypotension:** IV crystalloid 20 mL/kg (warmed), reassess; use inotropes if refractory  
    - **IV access:** two wide-bore lines (warm fluids)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
    - Monitor for ARDS (P/F ratio, CXR, O₂ saturations)  
    - Reassess for aspiration pneumonia – sputum culture, consider antibiotics if signs of infection  
    - Neurological monitoring (GCS, pupils) every hour – consider CT head if deterioration  
    - Repeat ABG after rewarming (metabolic acidosis may resolve)  
    - Maintain normothermia (avoid hyperthermia – brain damage risk)  
    - Electrolyte correction (especially K+, Mg²⁺)  
    - Anxiety/sedation if combative – consider benzodiazepines (watch respiratory depression)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
    - Respiratory follow-up for pulmonary fibrosis (rare)  
    - Neuropsychological assessment if prolonged hypoxia  
    - Cardiac follow-up if arrhythmia or troponin rise  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
    - Fluids: Warmed 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s at maintenance (30 mL/kg/day, adjust for fever) – avoid cold fluids  
    - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if no contraindication – hypothermia may impair clearance)  
    - Diet: NBM until airway reflexes assessed (if extubated, clear fluids then full diet)  
    - Observation: 4-hourly vital signs (core temp, SpO₂, GCS, urine output) – NEWS2 chart

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**  
    - *Antibiotics:* If aspiration pneumonia confirmed (fever, purulent sputum, new infiltrate): Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS [or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS – check local policy]  
    - *Bronchodilators:* Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (adult) PRN if wheeze – [dose per local protocol]  
    - *Rewarming:* Warm IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1L warmed to 40°C) – no specific drug dose  
- **Common alternatives:**  
    - *Antibiotics:* If penicillin allergy – levofloxacin 500 mg IV OD or [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
    - Hypothermia reduces drug clearance – adjust doses (e.g., enoxaparin, sedatives) cautiously  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
    - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose if CrCl <30  
    - Piperacillin-tazobactam: adjust for renal impairment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
    - **Rewarming too rapidly** (not a drug but a management error) – can cause rewarming shock, arrhythmias; target 0.5–1°C/hour  
    - **Giving cold IV fluids** – worsens hypothermia; always warm fluids before infusion

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
    - ARDS (2–48h) – high mortality  
    - Aspiration pneumonia (24–72h)  
    - Cerebral oedema / raised ICP (if prolonged hypoxia)  
    - Cardiac arrhythmia (hypothermia-related, reperfusion)  
    - Acute kidney injury (hypoperfusion, rhabdomyolysis)  
    - DIC (rare, from acidosis/hypothermia)  
- **Chronic:**  
    - Pulmonary fibrosis (rare, after severe ARDS)  
    - Neurocognitive deficits (memory, executive function)  
    - Persistent pulmonary function abnormalities (restrictive pattern)

### Prognosis
- Overall mortality ~15–30%; higher if submersion >5 min, core temp <28°C, GCS <5 on arrival, or need for CPR >25 min  
- Good neurological outcome: 50–80% in children with short submersion and rapid rewarming; adults worse  
- Surviving the first 24h → 80% eventual discharge (but chronic lung/neuro issues possible)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Assume spinal injury until proven** – immobilise all unconscious drowning patients (especially diving/fall)  
- **Rewarm aggressively but carefully** – aim 1–2°C per hour for severe hypothermia; use forced air, warm IV fluids, lavage if necessary  
- **Check blood glucose early** – hypoglycaemia worsen hypoxia; treat with IV 50% glucose 50 mL (caution in hypothermia – give warmed)  
- **Take a history of co-ingestions** – alcohol, drugs can mimic or cause drowning; toxicology screen mandatory if GCS not improving

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not stop resuscitation based on cold, dilated pupils** – hypothermia protects brain; continue ALS/rewarming until core >30°C  
- **Do not give steroids for aspiration** – NICE/RCUK recommends against dexamethasone/hydrocortisone; no proven benefit, may increase infection risk  
- **Do not give prophylactic antibiotics** – only start if clinical signs of infection (fever, purulent sputum, raised WCC, new infiltrate)  
- **Do not use cold fluids for IV access** – worsens hypothermia; always warm crystalloids

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
    - Shortness of breath worsening, fever, coughing up blood/purulent sputum  
    - New confusion, drowsiness, seizure, weakness  
    - Chest pain or palpitations  
- **Follow-up:**  
    - GP in 48–72h for respiratory assessment; if persistent cough/spO₂ <94% arrange outpatient CXR  
    - Consider referral to respiratory medicine (2-week wait if abnormal CXR at follow-up)

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU:**  
    - GCS <13 or ≥2-point drop (neurology review)  
    - SpO₂ <90% on 10 L/min O₂ or FiO₂ >0.5  
    - Need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation  
    - Core temp <32°C (ICU rewarming)  
    - Haemodynamic instability (BP <90/60, lactate >4 mmol/L)  
    - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained score ≥5 despite treatment  
- **When to call crash team:**  
    - Cardiac arrest (asystole is not unrecoverable – start ALS, continue rewarming)  
    - Seizure requiring airway protection  
    - Acute respiratory failure
